EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the significant snowfall that fell across the mountains on the morning of the 24th, an area of low pressure moving along the Gulf Coast spread yet another round of snow across the southern Appalachians and adjacent foothills during the evening of the 25th. The snow was heavy at times and quickly accumulated, with occasional mixed rain undercutting the totals a bit across the foothills. Heavy accumulations were reported in many areas by late evening. By the time the snow tapered off during the early morning of the 26th, total accumulations ranged from 2 to 6 inches, with locally higher amounts across the highest peaks.

Precipitation that initially began as a mixture of rain and sleet transitioned to freezing rain as temperatures cooled to freezing and below across the Georgia Piedmont. Freezing rain continued through the afternoon and evening, with accumulations primarily confined to elevated surfaces, including trees and power lines. Damaging ice accumulations were reported by late evening. Widespread ice accretion of one quarter to one half inch was reported, resulting in many trees and power lines falling and numerous to widespread power outages.

Light snow developed across the southern Appalachians during the evening of the 20th in association with a warm front. The snow began to mix with or change to sleet in some areas during the overnight before a transition to rain occurred around daybreak on the 21st. Accumulations ranged from a half inch to an inch, with locally higher amounts of around 2 inches in areas that saw only snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed across the Southern Appalachians along and immediately behind a strong arctic cold front that swept across the region during the afternoon of the 18th. Snow tapered off in most areas through the evening. Total accumulations generally ranged from a dusting up to an inch. Very strong winds resulted in considerable blowing and drifting of snow and periods of blizzard-like conditions across the highest elevations.

Light snow associated with a wave of low pressure overspread the southern Appalachians by late evening of the 23rd, and continued into the overnight. Snow, heavy at times, continued into pre-dawn hours of the 24th, when heavy snow accumulations were reported across the area. Total accumulations were generally in the 3 to 5 inch range, with locally higher amounts reported in the high elevations. The snow tapered off shortly after sunrise.

Sleet and snow overspread the mountains and foothills of northeast Georgia during the afternoon and began to accumulate. Precipitation changed quickly to sleet in most areas, before mixing with freezing rain from southwest to northeast during the late afternoon and early evening. Sleet and freezing caused deteriorating road conditions by early evening, when heavy accumulations of sleet and/or freezing rain were reported across much of the area. Most locations saw around a half inch to an inch of sleet, along with around a tenth of an inch of ice accretion. Roads became very treacherous and impassable in many areas until melting began on the afternoon of the 17th.

Marginal Risk (SPC) day produced rather widespread wind damage across west-central Georgia during the late afternoon hours of the 26th.  An EF-1 tornado was confirmed north of Warm Springs in Meriwether county.  An upper level wave moving east from AL/Gulf coast region spawned widespread convection that became an evident QLCS that tracked west to east across GA during the afternoon hours.  The most intense/severe storms were generally south of I-20 and west of I-75.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operator reported a power transformer and tree down on house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure built in to the region behind a cold front ushering in very cold temperatures and moderate winds leading to sub-zero wind chill values in the North Georgia Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brasstown RAWS site reported wind chill values below zero from around 6 PM EST on the evening of January 7th until around 8 AM EST on the morning of January 8th. The wind chill values remained below -10 degrees from around 9 PM EST on the 7th until after 7 AM EST on the 8th, bottoming out around 2 AM on the 8th when the air temperature of 1 degree combined with a 12 MPH wind to produce a wind chill of -16 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short wave troughs merged across the deep south as a surface low crossed the local area from the Gulf coast region. Cold 500 mb temps of -17 to -19 deg C supported elevated instability in the hail growth zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Mora Road and Victory Road. Witness may have also seen a gustnado in the field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low blow-out tides occurred during the early morning hours under strong offshore flow and along with low astronomical tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St. Simons Island tide gauge at Village Pier reported a blow-out low tide of -6.57 ft datum level NAVD88.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down near Radio Station Road. High end EF1 damage occurred along the path east of Johnson Lake Road along Georgia State Road 32. In this path the tornado severely damaged two mobile homes including a double wide trailer in which the walls were separated, the frame bent and flipped on its side. Large trees were snapped along the path. EF0 damage was noted along most of the path as the tornado tracked from Radio Station Road to McCarty as the storm approached State Road 32. The tornado continued northeast and weakened as it cross Ten Mile Creek over Mallard Road with a track ceasing near Bobwhite Road. The width of the tornado ranged between 30-50 yards. Two injuries occurred with one mobile home, and one resident was life-flighted with critical injuries. Both victims survived. The tornado was an EF1 with maximum winds of 100-110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually strong upper level winds for summer combined with high instability and steep mid level lapse rates to produce numerous reports of severe weather across the tri-state area.  Multiple rounds of severe weather occurred on June 30th, especially across the Florida Panhandle.  The city of Tallahassee reported that 160 tons of yard waste was generated from the severe weather, requiring extra work time to remove storm debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near Bubber Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the early afternoon hours ahead of a strong cold front supported by a upper level trough. The thunderstorms developed into line segments within a high shear, low surface-based instability environment. The thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts as well as a tornado that moved through an area west of downtown Savannah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado initially touched down near the intersection of Berwick Boulevard and Stonebridge Circle and moved northeast through the Stonebridge neighborhood. The tornado ripped numerous shingles and vinyl siding panels off a number of houses and damaged several large wooden fences. A gazebo was damaged and a trampoline was lifted and thrown for about a quarter of a mile. High winds forced rainwater through the walls of one home and damaged the drywall and carpet. Several large trees were blown over and snapped. Quite a bit of debris was noted hanging in the trees in the back of the neighborhood. One air conditioner unit was lifted and moved several feet.||The tornado then lifted and periodically touched down between Elk Road, Moose Jaw Road and Salt Creek Road for a total path length of three miles. In these areas, large trees were either blown down or snapped, including many large grand oak trees. Two homes were damaged by falling trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through the southern Appalachians during the morning and afternoon of the 7th, bringing strong winds and very cold air to the region. By late evening, sustained winds of 10 to 20 mph combined with air temperatures in the teens to yield wind chill values in the 0 to -5 range in the valleys. By daybreak on the 8th, air temperatures in the valleys were near 0 while the high elevations were well below 0. Wind chill values during this time ranged from 0 to -15 across the valleys, while stronger winds and colder temperatures likely yielded values as low as -50 across the high peaks. The very low wind chills abated throughout the 8th, as temperatures warmed and winds diminished. However, air temperatures remained below freezing throughout the 8th.

During their Sunday morning Service, church members quickly took cover under their pews when the wind became loud and their heavy front door was torn apart. The main damage occurred when the steeple fell to the ground.  Several shingles were stripped from the roof, and two church storage sheds were destroyed. Miraculously no one was injured. There were several more trees down with this tornado than the first one, including large pine trees (2-3 ft diameter) and large magnolia trees.  Max winds were estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong afternoon heating and a very moist airmass combined to produce a very unstable atmosphere by the afternoon of the 24th. A weak cold front and an outflow boundary from strong overnight storms across the Tennessee Valley pushed south into the state resulting in widespread strong to severe thunderstorms. Numerous reports of damaging thunderstorm winds and large hail were received, initially across North Georgia, during the afternoon and early evening before moving into portions of Central Georgia during the evening. Heavy rain associated with one severe thunderstorm produced isolated flash flooding in western portions of Gwinnett County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Putnam County 911 Center reported two trees down on power lines at the intersection of Pea Ridge Road and Flatrock Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Marginal Risk (SPC) day produced rather widespread wind damage across west-central Georgia during the late afternoon hours of the 26th.  An EF-1 tornado was confirmed north of Warm Springs in Meriwether county.  An upper level wave moving east from AL/Gulf coast region spawned widespread convection that became an evident QLCS that tracked west to east across GA during the afternoon hours.  The most intense/severe storms were generally south of I-20 and west of I-75.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down within a cluster of severe thunderstorms just west of Warm Springs /along Highway 194/ in southern Meriwether county. Much of the damage was to trees, either snapped or uprooted as the tornado tracked northeast before lifting just northwest of Woodbury. The worst of the damage occurred between Roosevelt Highway and Juke Line Road where numerous large trees were snapped or uprooted. Maximum winds of 90 MPH was determined based on this area and damage. [05/26/15: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF1, Meriwether, 2015:008].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong afternoon heating and a very moist airmass combined to produce a very unstable atmosphere by the afternoon of the 24th. A weak cold front and an outflow boundary from strong overnight storms across the Tennessee Valley pushed south into the state resulting in widespread strong to severe thunderstorms. Numerous reports of damaging thunderstorm winds and large hail were received, initially across North Georgia, during the afternoon and early evening before moving into portions of Central Georgia during the evening. Heavy rain associated with one severe thunderstorm produced isolated flash flooding in western portions of Gwinnett County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Employees at James O���Neal Chrysler-Dodge-Jeep reported quarter size hail at their location along U.S. Highway 27 north northwest of Bremen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon convection developed across southern Georgia with one storm becoming severe over Albany, producing a microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe storm produced a microburst over portions of northern Albany.  Numerous trees and power lines were blown down in a swath from Lovers Lane Road southwest to the Avalon United Methodist Church off Gillionville Road where the steeple was blown over.  Pictures from the area near Stonewater Drive showed trees down across neighborhood roads.  Some trees were blown down on houses and cars.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low associated with a deep upper-level trough lifted out of the lower Mississippi Valley and swept across the southern Appalachians the evening of January 3rd through the morning of January 4th producing heavy rain and flash flooding, severe thunderstorms and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS survey team found thunderstorm wind damage in north DeKalb County. Damage was first noted to trees along Shallowford Road near the North Fork of Peachtree Creek. Several trees were downed along Cosmos Drive N.E. with some damage to homes due to falling limbs. A large tree was downed on a home on Hawthorne Place with several other trees down surrounding the home. Trees were also downed along Jasmine Court and Canna Ridge. Extensive tree and home damage was noted along Braithwood Road and Applewood Court where numerous trees were downed on homes. All damage noted to structures was due to falling trees or branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low associated with a deep upper-level trough lifted out of the lower Mississippi Valley and swept across the southern Appalachians the evening of January 3rd through the morning of January 4th producing heavy rain and flash flooding, severe thunderstorms and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS survey team found that the same storm that produced a brief tornado in southern Pulaski County produced a new EF0 tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 85 MPH and a maximum path width of 100 yards, in eastern Pulaski County. The tornado touched down on Raleigh Cobb Road and traveled east-northeast for 2.18 miles before dissipating near Midway Church Road and Fire Road 246. A tin covered barn was blown over and minor damage was noted to the roof of a home. Several trees were downed along the path of the tornado. [01/04/15: Tornado #2, County #1-1, EF0, Pulaski, 2015:002].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low associated with a deep upper-level trough lifted out of the lower Mississippi Valley and swept across the southern Appalachians the evening of January 3rd through the morning of January 4th producing heavy rain and flash flooding, severe thunderstorms and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS survey team found that a brief EF0 tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 85 MPH and a maximum path width of 50 yards, touched down in far southern Pulaski County in the 1600 block of Pineview Highway. The tornado travelled .16 miles before dissipating. Approximately 20 small to medium-sized pine trees were snapped along the path of this tornado. A very large oak limb fell onto a brick outbuilding, causing minor damage to the roof and the covering was also blown off of a well house. [01/04/15: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF0, Pulaski, 2015:001].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low associated with a deep upper-level trough lifted out of the lower Mississippi Valley and swept across the southern Appalachians the evening of January 3rd through the morning of January 4th producing heavy rain and flash flooding, severe thunderstorms and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS survey team found that the same storm that produced two brief tornadoes in Pulaski County produced a brief EF1 tornado in central Dodge County. The tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 95 MPH and a maximum path width of 120 yards, touched down southwest of the intersection of Log Cabin Road and Antioch Church Road and traveled just 0.32 miles through the intersection before dissipating.  Around a dozen trees were downed narrowly missing homes and cars. [01/04/15: Tornado #3, County #1-1, EF1, Dodge, 2015:003].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the early afternoon hours ahead of a strong cold front supported by a upper level trough. The thunderstorms developed into line segments within a high shear, low surface-based instability environment. The thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts as well as a tornado that moved through an area west of downtown Savannah.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stage height on a USGS gage on the Allatoona Creek at Stilesboro Road near Mars Hill reached flood stage of 13 feet. The creek crest at 13.6 feet. Minor flooding occurred in the woodlands and fields near the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved through northeast Georgia during the morning and afternoon of the 7th, bringing gusty winds and very cold air to the area. By late evening, sustained winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with air temperatures in the teens to yield wind chill values near 0. Although winds gradually diminished overnight, air temperatures fell to around 10 degrees in many areas by daybreak, and wind chills of 0 to 5 above lingered until temperatures began warming during late morning. However, temperatures remained at or below freezing in many areas throughout the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued cold temperatures combined with a series of upper-level troughs and associated surface low pressure systems to bring significant snow totals to portions of North Georgia. Although light wintry precipitation occurred on the 24th, the heaviest snowfall amounts occurred from the afternoon of the 25th into the early morning hours of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Murray County Emergency Manager reported 5.0 inches of snow around Chatsworth with as much as 8.0 inches of snow in western portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the state on the 14th spreading cold and dry air across North Georgia. A surface low developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on the 15th sweeping abundant mid-level moisture over the state as it moved across the southeastern U.S. during the day on the 16th through the early morning hours of the 17th. Precipitation falling into the cold, dry air at the surface resulted in widespread freezing rain and drizzle across much of North Central and Northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dawson County Emergency Manager reported numerous trees and power lines down across the county due to up to a half inch of ice accumulation from freezing rain and drizzle. Over 50 percent of the county was without power by 730 PM EST with over 80 percent without power by the early morning hours on the 17th. Over 1000 trees were downed across the county by the time the ice melted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm produced an EF2 tornado that touched down in northern Burke County and traveled NNE into Richmond County.  Numerous trees were down and there was damage to homes, vehicles, and other property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey found an EF2 tornado touched down in northern Burke County taking down numerous trees then traveling NNE into Richmond County taking down trees, damaging homes, vehicles and other structures Done Roven Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and cold front moved through the area during the morning and early afternoon hours.  A squall line accompanied the front, and a few tornadoes and instances of straight line wind damage were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF1 tornado touched down.  A portion of a screened-in porch was blown down, and a large portion of the roof of a small, old, barn building was removed.  There were also some 8-12 inch diameter pine trees snapped at their trunks.  Max winds were estimated at 95 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Manager reported between one and three tenths of an inch of ice accumulation from freezing rain and drizzle across Cherokee County. The northeast portion of the county was hardest hit with numerous trees and power lines down and over 2000 residents without power from the evening of the 16th through the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With very cold air in place across the southeastern U.S., a fast moving upper trough and associated surface low brought a round of snow to North Georgia beginning during the late afternoon on the 20th and continuing overnight, before ending as light freezing drizzle during the day on the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County 911 Center reported two inches of snow across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County Emergency Manager reported between a tenth of an inch and a quarter of an inch of ice accumulation on trees, power lines and other elevated surfaces across the county due to freezing rain and drizzle. Over a half inch of ice accumulated on elevated surfaces in the southeast portion of the county. Numerous trees and power lines were downed across the county. Over 30000 residents were without power at the peak of the storm during the evening of the 16th through the morning of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the state on the 14th spreading cold and dry air across North Georgia. A surface low developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on the 15th sweeping abundant mid-level moisture over the state as it moved across the southeastern U.S. during the day on the 16th through the early morning hours of the 17th. Precipitation falling into the cold, dry air at the surface resulted in widespread freezing rain and drizzle across much of North Central and Northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A communications official with White County Fire/Rescue reported at least a quarter of an inch of ice accumulation on trees, power lines and other elevated surfaces due to freezing rain and drizzle. A trained spotter reported between a half inch and one inch of sleet. Numerous tree and power lines were downed and over 4000 residents were without power at the peak of the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 2.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered summertime convection developed during the afternoon hours with a few storms becoming severe.  Steep mid level lapse rates contributed to unusually large hail falling in Tift County with hen egg size hail reported.  Hail 2 or larger in diameter has only been reported 5 other times in the WFO Tallahassee forecast area during the month of June since 1955.  Those other dates were 6/5/88, 6/11/93, 6/10/95, 6/12/07, and 6/18/09.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near the intersection of Mt Pleasant Road and Lancaster Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the region on April 19th, bringing with it tornadoes and straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the long track tornado that started in Jackson county, FL and moved through Houston county, AL and into Early county, GA.  For the Early county, GA portion of the track, numerous trees were snapped or uprooted along its path.  Some damage to structures occurred, mainly with roofing material removed.  The damage width reached its maximum extent south of Blakely where the tornado was estimated to be around 500 yards wide.  Significant tree damage was noted about 2 miles south of Blakely just before the tornado started to lift.  Debris from this area was blown east of Highway 27, but the tornado itself did not cross the highway.  This section of the track was rated EF1 with winds around 105 mph.||While this is certainly not one of the stronger tornadoes to affect the WFO Tallahassee forecast area in recent years, this tornado is noteworthy for having a 39 mile long damage path occurring across three states.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front blasted through the southern Appalachians during the afternoon and evening of the 18th, bringing strong winds and bitterly cold air to the region. By mid-evening, sustained winds of 10 to 25 mph combined with air temperatures in the single digits and teens to yield wind chill values in the 0 to -10 range. By daybreak on the 19th, air temperatures in the valleys were near 0 while the high elevations were well below 0. Wind chill values during this time ranged from -5 to -20. The low wind chills continued throughout the 19th, as air temperatures failed to warm above the mid-20s in even the lowest elevations. Wind chills remained no higher than the single digits across most of the area until late morning on the 20th.

Overnight, sustained winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with air temperatures in the teens to yield wind chill values around 0 by daybreak on the 19th. Although winds diminished, air temperatures failed to warm above the 20s throughout the 19th, while record lows between 0 and 10 above were recorded the morning of the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the state on the 14th spreading cold and dry air across North Georgia. A surface low developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on the 15th sweeping abundant mid-level moisture over the state as it moved across the southeastern U.S. during the day on the 16th through the early morning hours of the 17th. Precipitation falling into the cold, dry air at the surface resulted in widespread freezing rain and drizzle across much of North Central and Northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation on signs, trees and other elevated surfaces in the Alpharetta area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually strong upper level winds for summer combined with high instability and steep mid level lapse rates to produce numerous reports of severe weather across the tri-state area.  Multiple rounds of severe weather occurred on June 30th, especially across the Florida Panhandle.  The city of Tallahassee reported that 160 tons of yard waste was generated from the severe weather, requiring extra work time to remove storm debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Ozell Road.

The city of Tallahassee reported that 160 tons of yard waste was generated from the severe weather, requiring extra work time to remove storm debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto power lines per a post to the WCTV facebook page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the state on the 14th spreading cold and dry air across North Georgia. A surface low developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on the 15th sweeping abundant mid-level moisture over the state as it moved across the southeastern U.S. during the day on the 16th through the early morning hours of the 17th. Precipitation falling into the cold, dry air at the surface resulted in widespread freezing rain and drizzle across much of North Central and Northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County Emergency Manager reported between a quarter of and inch and 4 tenths of an inch of ice accumulation on trees, power lines and other elevated surfaces due to freezing rain and drizzle. By late evening on the 16th over 30000 residents, around 80 percent of the county, were without power with numerous trees and power lines down. Several roads were closed due to downed trees blocking the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north of the Altamaha River basin placing the entire forecast area with the warm sector of an approaching mid level trough. A cluster of nearly stationary heavy rainfall producing storms lingered over Appling and Jeff Davis counties which caused extensive road washouts. Schools were closed in both counties one day due to poor road conditions after the heavy rainfall. Several roads were closed and impassable for a couple days due to areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of Appling county NE of Baxley were closed due to heavy flooding rainfall. Highway 144 NE of Baxley was impassable due to flood waters. Georgia Route 144 at Ellis Road had about 1 mile of water covering the road. The cost of property damaged was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data. This values is likely an under estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of persistent and strong east/northeast winds, the perigean spring tide and a full moon produced 2 days of elevated high tide cycles along the southeast Georgia coast. Major coastal flood stage levels were recorded at the Fort Pulaski, GA (FPKG1) tide gauge on Oct 27, 2015, which claimed 3rd place on the all-time historic crest list. Moderate coastal flood stage levels were also recorded at the Fort Pulaski, GA (FPKG1) tide gauge on Oct 28, 2015, which claimed 9th place on the all-time historic crest list.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager from Chatham County reported Highway 80 closed between Savannah and Tybee Island due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rain with scattered thunderstorms occurred across portions of southern Georgia on the evening of October 1st.  One storm near Omega in Tift county blew some trees down with one falling on a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Omega with one on the roof of a house.  There had been recent heavy rain of 2.5-3 inches in the area, and it is possible with the saturated ground that these trees fell in sub-severe winds, but that is not known.  Damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving area of upper level low pressure tapped into a tropical moisture feed associated with Hurricane Joaquin (located across the western Atlantic, well off the southeast coast) to produce periods of moderate to heavy rainfall across northeast Georgia throughout the 3rd and 4th. Storm total rainfall of 5 to 7 inches resulted in  saturated soils, which combined with wind gusts in excess of 20 mph to cause many trees to fall across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported multiple trees down in the Carnesville area due to a combination of saturated soil and gusty winds. One tree fell on a home on Highway 59.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow overridden by a passing upper level trough created locally heavy rainfall along portions of the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public measured about 3.3 inches of rainfall within about 3 hours on St. Simons Island. Standing water was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A full moon on Oct. 27th produced higher than normal astronomical tides and combined with moderate onshore flow created minor coastal flooding and beach erosion around the times of high tide. Water level departures were up 0.75 ft above predictions along the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tide gage at the St. Simons Island Village Pier measured a water level of 5.72 ft above NAVD88 datum. Minor coastal flooding typically begins at 5.5 ft above NAVD88.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A full moon on Oct. 27th produced higher than normal astronomical tides and combined with moderate onshore flow created minor coastal flooding and beach erosion around the times of high tide. Water level departures were up 0.75 ft above predictions along the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tide gage at Sea Camp dock on Cumberland Island measured at water level of 5.59 ft above NAVD88 datum. Minor coastal flooding typically begins at 5.0 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, non-thunderstorm, winds developed in a localized tight surface pressure gradient field behind an exiting low pressure center over portions of North Central and Northeast Georgia during the morning of October 4th. Several days of persistent moderate to heavy rain led to saturated soils which contributed to the widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Banks County Emergency Manager reported numerous trees blown across the county from several hours of sustained 30 to 35 MPH winds. Gusts were estimated over 40 MPH. Multiple roads were blocked and power lines downed.

Several roads were blocked by downed trees, including Cornelia Highway and Chandler Road, and power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed in an environment favorable for severe wet microbursts with high Wet Microburst Severity Index values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near Stage Road and Rupp Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front along with a moderately strong upper-level low combined to produce severe thunderstorms with numerous reports of damaging thunderstorm winds across North and Central Georgia during the afternoon and evening. Tropical moisture combined with the slow moving thunderstorms to produce heavy rain over portions of west central Georgia in the evening.  Around 3 inches of rain occurred in an hour to cause isolated flash flooding in the Columbus area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens County Emergency Manager reported several trees blown down from near Rentz to Dublin and Brewton, including on Mayberry Road, Highway 199 near I-16 and South Peachtree Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front combined with an upper-level short wave and strong afternoon heating to produce numerous severe thunderstorms, with damaging winds,  across North Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hall County Emergency Manager reported about a dozen trees blown down west of Murrayville. One tree fell on a mobile home on Crystal Lake Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture combined with slow moving thunderstorms to produce heavy rain over portions of North Georgia in the evening. Around 3 inches of rain occurred in one hour which caused some flash flooding in northeast Gordon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gordon County Emergency Manager reported some ditches and smaller creeks were overflowing their banks around 3 miles northeast of Redbud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of fast moving short waves swept over the region in the persistent westerly upper-level flow on the north side of a large ridge of high pressure centered over the Gulf of Mexico. With ample moisture and a stalled frontal boundary over the state, numerous thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage late in the evening of the 22nd and in the morning of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barrow County Emergency Manager reported wind damage to a barn on Dee Kennedy Road.  Some farm equipment was overturned and several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered summertime convection developed during the afternoon hours with a few storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Dillon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually strong upper level winds for summer combined with high instability and steep mid level lapse rates to produce numerous reports of severe weather across the tri-state area.  Multiple rounds of severe weather occurred on June 30th, especially across the Florida Panhandle.  The city of Tallahassee reported that 160 tons of yard waste was generated from the severe weather, requiring extra work time to remove storm debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down around Lake Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon convection developed across southern Georgia with one storm becoming severe over Albany, producing a microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down on Philema Road with wires blown down near the 2300 block of Beachview Drive as reported by WFXL-TV.  Damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed in an environment favorable for severe wet microbursts with high Wet Microburst Severity Index values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto Highway 3 heading towards Meigs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong instability and a strongly sheared environment set up favorable conditions for a damaging QLCS that swept through central and southeast Georgia. Reports of widespread wind damage occurred in the Savannah area along with a measured wind gust of 81 mph at the Savannah Airport and a measured 91 mph wind gust on an elevated crane at the Georgia Ports Authority.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported trees blown down at scattered locations along Skidaway Road between Virginia Avenue and 41st street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong instability and a strongly sheared environment set up favorable conditions for a damaging QLCS that swept through central and southeast Georgia. Reports of widespread wind damage occurred in the Savannah area along with a measured wind gust of 81 mph at the Savannah Airport and a measured 91 mph wind gust on an elevated crane at the Georgia Ports Authority.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported severe aircraft damaged by thunderstorm winds in the general aviation section of Savannah Airport. The Savannah ASOS measured a wind gust of 81 mph as the damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Marginal Risk (SPC) day yielded several severe thunderstorms in warm and unstable environment. Storms were slow-moving and training along southwest to northeast oriented lines during the afternoon and early evening hours. The torrential rainfall even destroyed a small lake in Coweta county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: HAM radio operator reported penny-sized hail about 3 miles north of Buford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong line of thunderstorms developed across central Georgia in the mid afternoon hours and pushed eastward through southeast Georgia in the late afternoon and early evening. The storms developed within a inland trough of low pressure with a upper shortwave trough helping to force the line. A highly unstable airmass with plentiful mid-level dry air created an environment supportive damaging wind gusts across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down in Rocky Ford by law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving strong to severe thunderstorms produced several inches of rain in a short period of time, resulting in flash flooding.  There were also reports of several trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over Highway 118 North near McClesky Mill.  In addition, water was filling ditches and overflowing onto Highway 45N and Scrap Israel Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front and a strong upper-level short wave combined to produce scattered severe thunderstorms across North and Central Georgia. Numerous reports of damaging thunderstorm winds with a few isolated reports of large hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Manager and the local news media reported trees down along I-20 near Glenwood Avenue and in Buckhead. One tree fell on a car in Buckhead, no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong afternoon heating and a very moist airmass combined to produce a very unstable atmosphere by the afternoon of the 24th. A weak cold front and an outflow boundary from strong overnight storms across the Tennessee Valley pushed south into the state resulting in widespread strong to severe thunderstorms. Numerous reports of damaging thunderstorm winds and large hail were received, initially across North Georgia, during the afternoon and early evening before moving into portions of Central Georgia during the evening. Heavy rain associated with one severe thunderstorm produced isolated flash flooding in western portions of Gwinnett County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walton County 911 Center reported trees and power lines blown down at New Hope Church Road and Highway 78 in Between and at Nunnally Farm Road and Highway 138 west southwest of Monroe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability, ample moisture and strong afternoon heating resulted in widespread strong thunderstorms over North and Central Georgia. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported quarter sized hail on Mauldin Circle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pine trees were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed in an environment favorable for severe wet microbursts with high Wet Microburst Severity Index values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was blown down near the intersection of Spring Flats Road and Greenleaf Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed during early-to-mid afternoon across the northeast Georgia mountains and foothills, then moved slowly south, impacting the Piedmont by late afternoon. Several of the storms produced brief severe weather in the form of damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light northerly wind regime focused the best afternoon convergence across inland SE GA where drier air from the north limited thunderstorm coverage but also contributed to gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in southern Wayne county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north of the Altamaha River Basin in advance of an approaching cold front and parent upper level trough pushing downstream of the MS river valley. Modest instability and shear was in place with supported scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Little Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level short wave trough over central Georgia shifted across the area during the late afternoon and into the evening. This forcing combined with additional shallow lift from the sea breezes and ahead of a weak surface trough over south Georgia and produced scattered severe storms over a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down NW of Kings Bay Naval Base.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported at the Walmart Supercenter in Bainbridge.

A strong short wave associated with an upper-level trough digging into the southeastern U.S. produced scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across North and Central Georgia during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained storm spotter reported hail up to the size of pennies just north of Marietta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of persistent and the approach of the full Super Moon of 2015 created higher than normal tides along the local coast with minor flooding impacts around the times of high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 8:15 pm on 9/26 the the USGS tide gage at the St. Simons Island Village Pier measured 5.25 ft above NAVD88 datum. This was 0.24 ft above the highest astronomical tide of 3.35 ft NAVD88. At 8:30 pm, the USGS tidal gage on the Brunswick River near the city of Brunswick measured 5.81 ft NAVD88. This was 0.21 ft above the highest astronomical tide of 5.6 ft NAVD88.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of persistent and the approach of the full Super Moon of 2015 created higher than normal tides along the local coast with minor flooding impacts around the times of high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS water level gage at Sea Camp Dock on Cumberland Island reached 5.3 ft above NAVD88. Highest astronomical tide is 4.7 ft above NAVD88.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low moving across southern Georgia swept deep moisture across North and Central Georgia during the day providing moderate instability over the region. A strong short wave associated with an upper-level trough digging into the southeastern U.S. produced scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across North and Central Georgia during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Polk County Emergency Manager reported several trees and power lines blown down in the vicinity of North College Street and West Girard Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, persistent upper-level ridge of high pressure resulted in warm afternoon temperatures across northwest Georgia. An infant left inside of a vehicle for several hours died of hyperthermia in Walker County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Chattanooga Tennessee television news station reported a heat related death in Chickamauga, Georgia in Walker County. An 11-month-old boy was left in a closed vehicle for approximately two and a half hours during the afternoon of Saturday, September 5th. Air temperatures were reported to be in the lower 90s and a Walker County Sheriff's Department official estimated that temperatures inside of the vehicle reached as high as 130-170 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of periods of persistent and strong northeast winds, the proxigean spring tides, and a full moon produced several days of elevated high tides. A few of the high tide cycles produced flooding along the southeast Georgia coast, especially in and around the Tybee Island area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The morning high tide reached 9.71 feet mean lower low water at the Fort Pulaski tide gauge and resulted in flooding on Highway 80. The road was closed with approximately 10 inches of water on the roadway near Lazaretto Creek, and there was also 4 to 6 inches of water reported across the road between mile markers 30 and 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of periods of persistent and strong northeast winds, the proxigean spring tides, and a full moon produced several days of elevated high tides. A few of the high tide cycles produced flooding along the southeast Georgia coast, especially in and around the Tybee Island area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The morning high tide peaked at 9.81 feet mean lower low water at the Fort Pulaski tide gauge. This produced flooding on Highway 80 causing the road to close near the Bull River bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of persistent and strong northeast winds, and elevated tides due to astronomical influences resulted in an enhanced risk of rip currents along the southeast Georgia coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chatham County Emergency Management reported that rip currents in the waters off of Tybee Island caused 3 fatalities and 2 injuries in a group of people during a religious celebration. First responders on the scene pulled 5 people out of the water and rushed 4 of them to Memorial University Medical Center. Two of those pulled from the water were pronounced dead at the hospital and crews searched area waters for a third person for 2 days following the event. This person was never found and was presumed deceased.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak but persistent upper-level trough over the eastern U.S. and ample tropical moisture combined to produce multiple rounds of precipitation over Central Georgia the evening of the 21st through the afternoon of the 22nd. A period of heavy rain during the early morning hours produced flash flooding in the city of Americus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sumter County Emergency Manager reported a few duplex homes in a low lying area off the 900 block of East Furlow Street in Americus were flooded when a nearby drainage ditch overflowed. Several people were evacuated from their homes. Around a dozen cars were inundated by the flood waters up to their door handles. The flood waters covered a portion of East Furlow Street. Another home at Matt Drive and Montgomery Street had four inches of water inside the house due to nearby storm drains unable to handle the rapid runoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak but persistent upper-level trough over the eastern U.S. and ample tropical moisture combined to produce multiple rounds of thunderstorms over North Georgia the evening of the 10th. A thunderstorm with very heavy rain produced some isolated flash flooding in southwest DeKalb County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stage height rapidly rose to a flood stage of 11 feet on a USGS stream gage around 6 miles southeast of Atlanta on the Intrenchment Creek.  The creek crest at 11.9 feet. Minor flooding occurred in the woodlands and natural flood plain of the creek upstream and downstream from the gage at Constitution Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and killed 10 cows standing under a tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten cows were killed when a pine tree they were standing under was struck by lightning.  According to the 2010 farming and ranch guide, the average value of a cow is  about $1.58 per pound.  The average cow weighs about 2,400 pounds.  Thus the damage loss is estimated at about $37,920.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a couple of days of occasional moderate to heavy rain acted to elevate stream flows and soil moisture levels, heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developing along and ahead of a cold front produced 2-3 inches of rain in just a few hours across portions of the Georgia Piedmont, resulting in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported minor flash flooding across the southern part of Elbert County, after around 2 inches of rain fell in just a few hours. Chastain Rd was closed, possibly due to flooding along a tributary of Wahachee Creek. Wahachee Creek also overflowed its banks and flooded Morgan Ln, briefly threatening a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a couple of days of occasional moderate to heavy rain acted to elevate stream flows and soil moisture levels, heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developing along and ahead of a cold front produced 2-3 inches of rain in just a few hours across portions of the Georgia Piedmont, resulting in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After around 2 inches of rain fell across much of Elbert County, a stream gauge on Beaverdam Creek in the far northern part of the county exceeded established flood stage, indicating flooding of Brickyard Rd and Horse Farm Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a warm front associated with an area of low pressure positioned to the west. A couple of these storms became strong to severe across southeast Georgia and produced damaging wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an elevated astronomical high tide due to a new moon cycle, and strong northeast winds from high pressure centered over New England helped to produce shallow coastal flooding along Highway 80 between Savannah and Tybee Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tybee Island Police Department reported that the eastbound lane of Highway 80 was closed from the Bull River to Tybee Island due to flooding. The morning high tide peaked at 9.49 feet Mean Lower Low Water at Fort Pulaski.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow and gradually warning astronomical high tides brought minor coastal flooding during the morning high tide cycle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tide gage at the St. Simons Island Village Pier measured 5.22 ft above NAVD88 datum levels. Minor coastal flooding typically begins at 5.0 above NAVD88.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the tri-state area with heavy rainfall and some severe weather.  In southern Georgia, there were 4 EF0 tornadoes confirmed with minor damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down near the intersection of Pine Level and Ridge Road in northern Grady county.  The primary damage was to pine trees with minor roof damage to a mobile home.  Law enforcement witnessed the tornado.  This tornado was rated EF0 with max winds around 75 mph.  Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the tri-state area with heavy rainfall and some severe weather.  In southern Georgia, there were 4 EF0 tornadoes confirmed with minor damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado produced minor damage to vinyl siding and metal roofing to one home.  No other damage was reported.  The Doerun police also reported a funnel cloud in the vicinity occasionally rising and dropping.  This tornado was rated EF0 with max winds around 65 mph.  Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Once again Georgia found itself stuck under persistent northwesterly upper-level flow for several days. A series of short waves combined with strong daytime heating to produce isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms each day, mainly in the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County 911 Center reported several trees blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing along the Gulf Coast spread very moist air and rain into north Georgia early on the 1st, and continuing through much of the 2nd and the early part of the 3rd. Three-day rainfall totals of 4 to 5 inches resulted in some flooding across portions of Elbert County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms and automated stream gauges indicated flooding developed across northern portions of Elbert County after more than 4 inches of rain fell in 36 hours, with much of that falling on the morning of the 2nd. A gauge on Beaverdam Creek exceeded its established flood stage, indicating flooding of Brickyard Rd and Horse Farm Rd. County comms also reported water over the bridge on Brown Brothers Rd. Amberly Rd was also flooded by water from a tributary of Van Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an elevated astronomical high tide due to a new moon cycle, and strong northeast winds from high pressure centered over New England helped to produce shallow coastal flooding along Highway 80 between Savannah and Tybee Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tybee Island Police Department reported that Highway 80 was closed from Bull Island to Tybee Island due to flooding. The morning high tide peaked at 9.49 feet Mean Lower Low Water at Fort Pulaski.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the tri-state area with heavy rainfall and some severe weather.  In southern Georgia, there were 4 EF0 tornadoes confirmed with minor damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very brief tornado touched down near Union Church Road southwest Miller county.  The tornado heavily damaged a chicken coop, pulled vinyl siding off of a house, displaced a trampoline, and brought down large tree limbs.  The tornado was rated EF0 with max winds around 70 mph.  Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level trough swept across the region during the day. Across central Georgia, south of a persistent stationary frontal boundary, the atmosphere was able to become moderately unstable. Strong shear and the moderate instability combined to produce a severe thunderstorms with two short-lived tornadoes in central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS survey team determined that a tornado occurred in southern Telfair county. The EF1 tornado with estimated maximum winds around 94 MPH began along Raymond Bloodworth Barkley West Road between County roads 54 and 52 near China Hill and travelled 1.63 miles east northeast before ending along State Highway 117 near China Hill Road east of China Hill. The tornado snapped 15 to 20 hardwood trees and caused structural damage to a building lifting the roof off. No injuries were reported. [11/02/15: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF1, Telfair, 2015:009].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough and strong short wave moved through the region during the day. Strongly diffluent upper-level flow combined with a sharp cold front and steep mid-level lapse rates to produce widespread severe thunderstorms across north Georgia with numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported quarter size hail on Mansell Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough and strong short wave moved through the region during the day. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level trough swept across the region during the day. Across central Georgia, south of a persistent stationary frontal boundary, the atmosphere was able to become moderately unstable. Strong shear and the moderate instability combined to produce a severe thunderstorms with two short-lived tornadoes in central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS survey team determined that a tornado occurred in southeastern Telfair county. The EF1 tornado with estimated maximum winds around 87 MPH began south of State Highway 134, west of Hugh McBride Road, and travelled .77 miles east northeast before ending along State Highway 134 east of Hugh McBride Road. The tornado snapped or uprooted numerous softwood and hardwood trees and caused structural damage to two center-pivot irrigation systems. No injuries were reported. The Telfair County 911 center reported a funnel cloud visible with this storm further east as it crossed U.S. Highway 341 north of Lumber City. Local law enforcement stopped traffic on the highway as the funnel cloud moved east, however they reported that there was no indication the circulation ever reached the ground and no damage was reported or found in this area. [11/02/15: Tornado #2, County #1-1, EF1, Telfair, 2015:010].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure building into the region from the northeast and a deep surface low over the northern Gulf of Mexico resulted in a tight surface pressure gradient across north Georgia on the morning of November 9th with subsequent gusty winds. These gusty winds combined with saturated soils from several days of rainfall resulted in a few large trees blown down across Fulton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Manager reported a few trees blown down across Fulton County due to non-thunderstorm winds, including one tree down on Holly Road at Ezra Church Drive NW and another blocking lanes on Moreland Avenue SE. One man was killed when his vehicle struck a tree that had fallen across Woodhaven Road NW in the Buckhead area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through north and central Georgia resulted in scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon into the early evening. Isolated severe thunderstorms produced a few reports of damaging winds and large hail in the Atlanta metropolitan area and portions of central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Manager reported trees blown down from Piedmont Park to Emory University and Wesley Woods. A tree was down on a power line at 10th Street and Monroe drive. Several trees were down in Wesley Woods. On the Emory University campus several trees were down, one on a fraternity house on Eagle Row Road, and power outages were reported from downed power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KEOX dual-pol radar confirmed a brief tornado touchdown north of Morgan with an unambiguous tornadic debris signature.  The only damage was to trees with no structural damage was reported in this very rural area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough and associated strong cold front pushed through the region. Although instability was limited, strong deep-layer wind shear resulted in a few severe thunderstorms and tornadoes in parts of north Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local Atlanta television news station reported a man injured by a lightning strike near Meadow Lark after lightning struck a nearby power line and travelled through the ground to where the man was standing. The injuries were reported to be minor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cobb County 911 center reported several trees down from Kennesaw to Smyrna, including one down on Cooper Lake Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough and associated strong cold front pushed through the region. Although instability was limited, strong deep-layer wind shear resulted in a few severe thunderstorms and tornadoes in parts of north Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS survey team found that a second EF1 tornado developed from the same thunderstorm that produced the earlier Coweta County tornado. This new tornado touched down near Creekside High School where minor damage occurred to the goal posts and bleachers at the football field. Around the Durham Lakes Country Club, several large pine trees were uprooted as the tornado became better organized. One minor injury was reported in this area when a tree fell on a car near the country club. Near the intersection of Virlyn B. Smith road and Dynasty Drive there was a 120-yard wide area where numerous large trees were snapped and uprooted around several homes. The homes sustained only very minor roof and/or siding damage; however there was a small, unsecured, shed that was ripped apart by the winds in this location. Along Tell Road, additional large trees, mainly pines, were snapped near their bases. |The tornado continued to move northeast snapping trees along Rivertown Road just north of Clarence Duncan Park. Along White Bird Way, which turns into Tall Deer Drive, trees were snapped and uprooted with several homes sustaining significant damage from falling trees. Similar damage was seen along Small Elk Court. Maximum wind speeds were estimated to be around 95 mph with a maximum width of 200 yards. The tornado rapidly weakened and lifted before reaching West Campbellton Street just north of the town of Fairburn. [11/18/15: Tornado #2, County #1-1, EF1, Fulton, 2015:012].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough and associated strong cold front pushed through the region. Although instability was limited, strong deep-layer wind shear resulted in a few severe thunderstorms and tornadoes in parts of north Georgia.

Structural damage occurred at two locations along Hammermill Road. The front part of a north-facing wall was ripped apart at one location while across the street part of the roof and metal siding on the front of another building was peeled back. Just behind the first building, two pine trees were uprooted. Maximum wind speeds were estimated at 75 MPH. [11/18/15: Tornado #3, County #1-1, EF0, DeKalb, 2015:013].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous upper level pattern developed across the country with a large upper level trough remaining nearly stationary west of the area for several days. Continuous southwest flow brought unseasonably warm temperatures and high moisture content across the area. Excessive rainfall amounts occurred during the last week of the month with amounts in excess of 15 inches falling across the upper portions of the Pea, Choctawhatchee, and Chattahoochee River basins with lesser but still significant amounts extending eastward into central Georgia. This resulted in significant river flooding downstream in southwest Georgia across the Chattahoochee, Flint, Kinchafoonee, and Muckalee Creek basins.  The flooding along much of the Flint River basin crested in early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred within the Kinchafoonee Creek basin as well as the lower portions of the Muckalee Creek basin.   The Kinchafoonee crested at its 3rd highest stage on record, causing significant damage to homes in the area, especially along Century Road.  Some homes on North Hampton Road also received flooding from Muckalee Creek.  The damage cost was estimated.

In minor flood stage, approaches to Oakridge Drive bridge flooded, and flooding reached the lowest elevations along Third Avenue, Highland Avenue, and Front Street.  This flooding continued into early January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous upper level pattern developed across the country with a large upper level trough remaining nearly stationary west of the area for several days. Continuous southwest flow brought unseasonably warm temperatures and high moisture content across the area. Excessive rainfall amounts occurred during the last week of the month with amounts in excess of 15 inches falling across the upper portions of the Pea, Choctawhatchee, and Chattahoochee River basins with lesser but still significant amounts extending eastward into central Georgia. This resulted in significant river flooding downstream in southwest Georgia across the Chattahoochee, Flint, Kinchafoonee, and Muckalee Creek basins.  The flooding along much of the Flint River basin crested in early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor river flooding along the Flint River extended down to the Newton area during the last few days of December.  The bulk of the flooding occurred in early January as the river crested at its 3rd highest crest on record in the moderate flood category.  Several roads were flooded and closed.  This flooding continued into January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent, deep and strong southwesterly upper-level flow across the eastern U.S.  resulted in an extremely moist and moderately unstable atmosphere over North and Central Georgia. A stalled frontal boundary and a series of short waves in the southwesterly upper flow resulted in multiple rounds of heavy rain, and strong to severe thunderstorms, with widespread flash flooding, damaging thunderstorms winds, hail and an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A USGS stream gauge on Brushy Fork Creek at Beaver Road or 3 miles west to southwest of Loganville reached flood stage of 9 feet. The creek crest at 9.7 feet. Minor flooding occurred in the woodlands and fields upstream and downstream from the gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southwesterly flow aloft across the eastern U.S. resulted in an extremely moist and moderately unstable atmosphere over North Georgia. A slow moving cold front with a series of short waves passing over the front resulted in multiple rounds of heavy rain with areas of flooding. Several debris flows of saturated mountainous terrian in Gilmer County blocked roads with mud. Some other roads and culverts were washed out due to the constant rapidly flowing high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches occurred in less than 12 hours over most of Gilmer County during the night and early morning hours. The heavy rain caused debris flows of mud which covered four roads and washed out four other roads. An additional 12 other roads were flooded for several hours. The flood waters from Cherry Log Creek surrounded three homes off of Joann Sisson Road which caused some evacuations. Water did not get into the three houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving short wave and its associated cold front produced an isolated severe thunderstorm with a brief tornado in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found a short tornado path with EF1 damage from wind speeds estimated at 90 mph. Multiple large tree limbs were downed with one large tree uprooted. The path started just west of I-59, crossed the interstate and ended just east of the interstate along State Route 11 near Cloverdale Road. A residential neighborhood was without power and a few houses had shingle and siding damage. [04/03/15: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF1, Dade, 2015:004].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave and moderate instability combined to produce widespread thunderstorms across north and central Georgia throughout the daytime on the 19th into the early morning hours of the 20th. Scattered severe thunderstorms with damaging winds occurred across the area. A warm front lifting north across central Georgia during the late morning and early afternoon provided sufficient wind shear to produce several short-lived tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ft. Benning Emergency Manager reported that a tornado moved across the Chattahoochee River from Alabama into Georgia and tracked across southern Ft. Benning. The tornado moved over mainly undeveloped, forested areas of Ft. Benning where numerous trees were downed. Based on information from the Emergency Manager and a NWS survey team from Birmingham, it was determined that the intensity was EF-1 with maximum wind speeds of 100 MPH and an average path width of 350 yards. [04/19/15: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF1, Chattahoochee, 2015:005].
